Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.

step2_rag_chunks: A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
